channel.title: Open Source on Ivan Fraixedes' Blog
channel.description: Recent content in Open Source on Ivan Fraixedes' Blog
channel.copyright: This works is license under a Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike 4.0 International license

item.title: My 5 favourite tweets of the week #8
item.description:
Another short week is almost done and here my 5 favourite tweets of the last 7 days which appeared in my timeline.

The moment arrived, after several years under development, Rust beta has been released and the first stable release has been scheduled for 15th of May, so AWESOME

Rust 1.0-beta is here!! http://t.co/8HTh1jrL69

— Rust Language (@rustlang) April 3, 2015

Very nice web resource to see what ES6 brings and how these new features differ from its predecessor ES5

nice hands-on resource for learning ES6: http://t.co/VB5WcTT4LR

— Ilya Grigorik (@igrigorik) April 4, 2015

I’ve go quite old great blog post about Open Source and how delaying to make it open affects, at least when it’s created by a governmental organisation

another great post by @kfogel: Be Open [Source] From Day One http://t.co/8hTihlTZNo

— Aidan Feldman (@aidanfeldman) April 8, 2015

A friend of mine tweeted about [Ghost](https://ghost.org/) blog post where they’ve made public all their revenues metrics, I’m very thrilled how some companies, as Buffer 1 2, and Baremetrics 1 2 3, are moving toward the transparency

$411,000 in ARR and @TryGhost just made all of their revenue metrics completely public: http://t.co/SGH5CFmeQE

— BROLΛПDO (@orliesaurus) April 9, 2015

Javascript is moving to take over the stablished Java and .NET enterprise stacks, we’ll see what will happen

JavaScript will lead a massive shift in enterprise development http://t.co/07XVzu9LgK

— Paul Krill (@pjkrill) April 9, 2015

Have a great weekend!

item.title: My 5 favourite tweets of the week #6
item.description:
Yep, this week my timeline came up with these marvelous tweets.

An awesome tool that take source code TODO comments and create Github issues to remind you that they have to be fixed in not to leave those there for ages

nifty tool to convert your TODO comments into Github issues: http://t.co/M2gwKz5RvI - bonus: install as a git precommit hook!

— Ilya Grigorik (@igrigorik) March 24, 2015

Opinions are opinions and the battles about preferences between techies is the day to day, don’t miss this

A Few Notes On Choosing Between Go and D for a Quick Project: http://t.co/KfO2C0svlb

— Go Newsletter (@golangweekly) March 21, 2015

Load a web site fast is one of the biggest headache of any web developer, here you can read a new technique that hopefully will help you to get less crazy each time that you must do it

New JavaScript Techniques for Rapid Page Loads: http://t.co/hZJD4LX6Ur

— JavaScript Daily (@JavaScriptDaily) March 21, 2015

Well if you were desiring that Blink implement pointer events, this tweet is good news for you

Based on feedback from developers & the Pointer Event Working Group, Blink is now reconsidering Pointer Events: https://t.co/8uARlPNoVp

— Chrome Developers (@ChromiumDev) March 25, 2015

One example which shows that the decisions are made mustn’t be forever, things change and some that was ideal in past doesn’t have to be idea for now or for the future

«This … makes me wonder what other standard unix programs make design assumptions that aren’t true in 2015…» — this. http://t.co/LpnWxVIXP1

— Michael Klishin (@michaelklishin) March 5, 2015

I hope that you have a great weekend.

item.title: My 5 favourite tweets of the week #5
item.description:
The last 7 days, twitter hasn’t only helped me to discover new libraries, techniques, tutorials, etc; it has also brought a quite interesting quote which I agree and cool tool which allows you record your terminal, let’s see them in appearing order

A practical video which show you some tips in how to create Go packages successfully, don’t miss to watch it if you are in the wave of Golang(http://golang.org)

Practical Tips for Creating a Go Package Successfully: https://t.co/PVdRndZIEM #video

— Go Newsletter (@golangweekly) March 14, 2015

Through a reply of a tweet which asked for tools to make terminal tutorial animations, I’ve discovered asciinema a wonderful Open Source tool to record your terminal

@robbyrussell https://t.co/cdNu1UqJFI is nice

— Frank Denis (@jedisct1) March 14, 2015

What about debugging Go in GDB, if you don’t know, then you can read this quick tutorial

Debugging Go in GDB: http://t.co/QaszV7pGiT (A quick tutorial on debugging a simple Go program using the popular GNU debugger.)

— Go Newsletter (@golangweekly) March 15, 2015

As I’ve commented in the introduction of this post, the mentioned quote is

Working hard for something… pic.twitter.com/3Ncjhys2SY

— Juergen R. Bolz (@123top10) March 15, 2015

And the last selected one is about how to stress your servers, do you thing that your servers are gods? maybe you want to know their limits

How To Stress Test CPU and Memory (VM) On a Linux and Unix With Stress-ng (via @Pocket) http://t.co/si9jQ3YkH3

— Paul B(ü|ue)tow (@plantanran) March 18, 2015

I hope that your week has been not too bad and you have a wonderful weekend.

item.title: My 5 favourite tweets of the week #4
item.description:
Another 7 days almost gone, hence there it goes the 5 tweets that I’ve selected from my Twitter timeline.

Thanks to @nixcraft I’ve discovered SSHFS, a network file system on top of SSH

How To Mount Remote Directory With SSHFS on a Linux http://t.co/O0G8MUsXoC #OpenBSD #OpenSSH #CentOS #Ubuntu #Debian pic.twitter.com/t3YYVa5W5w

— nixCraft (@nixcraft) March 7, 2015

Congratulations to io.js community to release another version v1.5.1

io.js bugfix release v1.5.1 out now @ https://t.co/MKaxWFC8Bn CHANGELOG @ https://t.co/JwuzQ2hV3B

— Rod Vagg.js (@rvagg) March 9, 2015

Mozilla released another post related with performance, this time related with SIMD.js (Single Instruction Multiple Data)

The State of SIMD.js Performance in Firefox: https://t.co/I4aHZ9SSTv

— JavaScript Daily (@JavaScriptDaily) March 10, 2015

Nice Open Source (MIT license) Golang library about arbritrary-precision fixed-point decimal library, which has been implemented and used in production for 6 months by Spring

Decimal: An Arbitrary-Precision Fixed-point Decimal Library - http://t.co/Cx3Dm8kPKa

— Go Newsletter (@golangweekly) March 12, 2015

Nice post of a great engineering work by LinkedIn to reduce the traffic of their websites over HTTP 1.1

great writeup from LinkedIn on SDCH.. "average additional compression across our static content was about 24%" @ http://t.co/Vm2XRPfWLc

— Ilya Grigorik (@igrigorik) March 12, 2015

Enjoy your weekend hacking around!

item.title: My 5 favourite tweets of the week #3
item.description:
This week I got my 5 favourite tweets of my twitter timeline almost the last two days; I’ve also been very busy this week, however I walked through in my timeline at the end of the each day and I’ve selected:

io.js team is working very hard, the open governance applied to the project, one of the main reason they forked NodeJS, seems that works quite well and they’re moving forward pretty fast; this tweet talks itself about it

io.js v2.x is probably closer than you think https://t.co/mXYpmyRQW0

— Rod Vagg.js (@rvagg) March 4, 2015

Quite amazing MIT open source project witch brings the ability to compile and execute c code in one go

C – Compile and execute C “scripts” in one go https://t.co/Q7pzgO5Ku9

— Hacker News (@newsycombinator) March 4, 2015

I’ve discovered parse5, an npm module released under MIT as well, which I considered that it looks interesting

Parse5: WHATWG HTML5-Compliant HTML Parsing/Serialization Toolset for Node - http://t.co/atODyW5Y0m

— JavaScript Daily (@JavaScriptDaily) March 4, 2015

One disclosed bug in OpenSSL always is quite interesting due the number of apps, systems, libraries, etc, which use it, however the versions with this issues are older than the version used by io.js and NodeJS production releases (0.12.0 and 0.10.36)

Are #nodejs and #iojs affected by the “FREAK Attack” OpenSSL vulnerability? http://t.co/DQi5sgDUev

— StrongLoop (@StrongLoop) March 3, 2015

And lastly I’ve selected a tweet which brought me to read a research experiment done by Mozilla; definitely, it deserves to have a read

The Emterpreter: Running Code Before It Can Be Parsed - https://t.co/MYU8VuElOn

— JavaScript Daily (@JavaScriptDaily) March 3, 2015

And that’s it of my part, have you got any interesting tweet that you’d like to share? drop it in a comment, I would like to read it.

item.title: My 5 favourite tweets of the week #2
item.description:
This week I’ve been delighted of

Rust lang released a new version 1.0.0-alpha.2, it’s the language that I want to take to write some lines of code, however until that moment, I’m following the evolution and reading some tips that I collect from the community

Rust 1.0.0.alpha.2 http://t.co/S72HcYo6t3 SO FREAKING EXCITING! #rustlang

— Community Opener (@flaper87) February 20, 2015

I’ve got the chance to have a read to an amazing technical blog post about how to reduce Go allocations which drove me to read other two posts in golang blog which allowed me to learn more about the internals

Reducing Allocations in Go Code: http://t.co/ODHknUsNU3

— Go Newsletter (@golangweekly) February 22, 2015

I’ve discovered a nice so nice open source service implemented in Go, Gogs, a self-hosted Git service

Gogs: A Self-Hosted Git Service Written in Go - http://t.co/juisUGaCPN (Think GitHub-lite.)

— Go Newsletter (@golangweekly) February 23, 2015

I could know the tradeoffs of why a big corporate, as Walmart, decide to run open source software, reading the first post of a new series

Why we run an open source program - Walmart Labs http://t.co/Ihy9dN2YLs via HN

— nixCraft (@nixcraft) February 25, 2015

iojs released a new version 1.4.1 with delightful promise/process, streams with TLS, new event for http, v8, libuv & npmjs update; the community behind this NodeJS fork never ceases to amaze us with their open governance model

io.js v1.4.1 is out @ https://t.co/MKaxWFkxcN incl new "unhandledRejection" & "rejectionHandled" events and more: https://t.co/9LPbP6VQxu

— Rod Vagg.js (@rvagg) February 26, 2015

I hope that you could enjoy with some of them as I did.

Have a lovely geeky weekend.

item.title: My 5 favourite tweets of the week #1
item.description:
Today, Friday, I’ve decided to start this serie of weekly posts where I will publish my 5 favourite tweets that I’ll have got from my twitter timeline since Friday of previous week until Thursday of the current week.

You should find here every Friday, by 1pm a post with those 5 tweets that I’ve selected, so to check it by Friday afternoon or in your geeky weekend to discover something new that you may have missed.

This week, I’ve selected

Something cool with watchify

more experiments with "script injection" in Chrome, much faster with watchify https://t.co/6MUwWsNPRZ / @substack @dan_abramov

— Matt DesLauriers (@mattdesl) February 15, 2015

iojs is looking forward to adding HTTP2

With Chrome ditching SPDY and going full on HTTP2, io.js may follow with core HTTP2 support, discussion here: https://t.co/pVcyyQ1Vyw

— Rod Vagg.js (@rvagg) February 10, 2015

A very good and deep technical post about the final semantics of ECMAScript 6 classes

Classes in ECMAScript 6: The Final Semantics - http://t.co/56RQIkUYAN

— JavaScript Daily (@JavaScriptDaily) February 17, 2015

Being aware about security stuff is never time wasted, then check those 10 immutable Laws of Security

10 immutable Laws of Security: http://t.co/WDj66fXI9A - great read, lots of hard won wisdom.

— Ilya Grigorik (@igrigorik) February 18, 2015

A very nice tool to analyse web logs, written in golang

GoAccess – Terminal-Based Visual Web Log Analyzer - http://t.co/VnwgBT9pxP #golang #sysadmin (cc @nixcraft)

— Nabil ZADVAT (@NabilZADVAT) February 18, 2015

I hope one of them was in your interests.

Have a nice weekend.

item.title: GitEvents a New Community Open Source Project
item.description:
Saturday of a couple weeks ago I spent my time in a hackday which ran simultaneously in two places Barcelona hosted by NodeBCN and London hosted by LNUG.

Hackathon had the aim to kickstart a new community Open Source project initially called gitup owned by BarcelonaJS Github organisation however due the incredible collaboration that it got by the attendees it’s become an itself organisation called GitEvents hosting the project and a bunch of dependant projects which must end up with the first known requirements of the project.

I had a very good experience seeing how it was born and how it is growing up.

Here I’m going to tell you its first steps:

The idea

The idea of the project is backed by @PatrickHeneise with the main aim to automatise and drop down the costs as much as possible the administration of managing Tech meetups using GitHub Issues as an interface and GitHub API to fire automatic actions which can be without any specific order update the group website, send a tweet, send an email, etc.

How the Idea spread

I had briefly heard @PatrickHeneise’s idea when I attended to BarcelonaJS Meetup when I was visiting my family for Christmas time, however it was just that, I knew that he was interested in developing it and other NodeJS groups as BerlingJS were interested as well.

Without knowing anything more about it, on 6th of January I saw a Patrick’s tweet, with less 140 characters ;D, announcing the project and asking for help.

Working with the GitHub API to create/update a JSON file for github-meetup integration. #helpwanted https://t.co/6sdvTdtz75

— Patrick Heneise (@PatrickHeneise) January 6, 2015

Nice, I decided to retweet it and the magic started; @iancrowther expressed his interest in the project and I added sugar on it.

@iancrowther, Yes, it is, @PatrickHeneise is the BCN Javascript and @nodejs Meetup group organiser, I've been in touch in person on Xmas

— Ivan Fraixedes (@ifraixedes) January 7, 2015

and after the introductions, the conversation followed in Gitter.

@PatrickHeneise @ifraixedes lets move this to gitter: https://t.co/WwmHgUzkhT

— ツ Ian Crowther (@iancrowther) January 7, 2015

That conversation brought the idea, by Ian, to organise a hackathon which became a hackathon running in both cities Barcelona and London

The Hackathon

The hackathon was announced and some people joined in both places, there wasn’t a massive number of attendees however people who attended was quite exited about the project and wanted to work in, or at least getting start with NodeJS.

Then the project started to have a community contribution; people followed some documentation and opened issues, with tasks to do, which Patrick and Ian created on the previous days.

Patrick and Ian were doing some skype calls during the day to know in realtime how the things were going and keeping the two different places in communication, hence transmitting the spirit of the hackathon, +1 guys.

At the end it was a day of community; some contributors were working in one of the plugins, others in the core and the outcomes were some pull requests sent and merged, more issues opened, some specifications and decisions and so on, pretty amazing to have seen.

Post Hackathon

After the day of the hackathon, some of the initial contributors are working on; some of them have been working since the following Sunday and so far the project has:

  • 9 repositories which are in development, 1 is the core and 8 are plugins.
  • 8 people, 2 owners and 6 contributors.

There are several issues opened and people discussing in them the most of the tagged as “inprogress” and “enhancement”

The biggest challenge starts now, keeping the engagement and the interests of the current contributors, encourage new ones, create interest for the project and create a bigger community.

The Wrap Up

I’m really happy to see how an Open Source community starts, even though it is in very early stage Patrick and Ian are very keen to move the project forward and grow a passionate community.

Being a collaborator, I’ve learnt how a community Open Source project is being organised, not just the good things but also all the pains, because organise a distributed team where everybody have opinion is not easy, however very rewarding.

Hopefully I will keep myself involved on it and make some new contributions as well.

And you, are you interested in getting involved?

don’t hesitate to visit GitEvents and take a look to the current issues, see if you can help in any, or just make suggestions, improvements and report bugs whenever the first usable release appear.

See you in the next post.
